items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Pavlov's dogs salivating to a bell is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Classical conditioning. “Operant conditioning” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who carried out the Bobo doll study in 1961?
items.why: Correct answer: Bandura. “Skinner” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Little Albert study published?
items.why: The real figure is 1920 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
